hero.headline: Finance does not need an LLM everywhere, but governance has to come first
hero.headline_html: Finance does not need an LLM everywhere, but governance has to come first
hero.subtitle: This paper does not offer a benchmark win or a fresh dataset. It offers something more useful for regulated firms, a six-decision framework for deciding when an LLM belongs in finance, what safeguards it needs, and when a simpler system should stay in charge.

top_block.label: KEY CONTRIBUTION
top_block.body: Tavasoli and colleagues argue that financial firms should treat LLM adoption as a governance decision before they treat it as a model choice. Their six-step framework starts by asking whether an LLM is necessary at all, then forces teams through data governance, risk, ethics, ROI, and deployment choices before anything reaches production.

sections.label: The real argument
paragraphs: The paper is conceptual rather than experimental, so its contribution is not a new result on accuracy or latency. Instead, it lays out a six-decision workflow for financial institutions that want the upside of language models without stumbling into a compliance mess they then have to explain to auditors.

That first decision matters more than most AI strategy decks admit. If a task depends on messy, contextual language, such as reading disclosures, triaging customer messages, or interpreting long documents, an LLM may earn its keep. If the task mainly needs consistency, traceability, and deterministic outputs, the authors say a rule-based or conventional NLP system will often do the job better and with less drama.
paragraphs_html: The paper is conceptual rather than experimental, so its contribution is not a new result on accuracy or latency. Instead, it lays out a six-decision workflow for financial institutions that want the upside of language models without stumbling into a compliance mess they then have to explain to auditors.

That first decision matters more than most AI strategy decks admit. If a task depends on messy, contextual language, such as reading disclosures, triaging customer messages, or interpreting long documents, an LLM may earn its keep. If the task mainly needs consistency, traceability, and deterministic outputs, the authors say a rule-based or conventional NLP system will often do the job better and with less drama.

sections.label: Why governance dominates the framework
paragraphs: Most of the framework sits upstream of deployment. The paper tells firms to classify data sensitivity, check privacy law and data-localisation constraints, and decide what processing is allowed before model selection turns into procurement theatre. In finance, that order is not bureaucratic fussiness. It is the difference between a pilot and a formal incident.

The safeguard layer is broad but concrete. The paper lists controls such as data minimisation, federated learning, anonymisation, encryption, secure enclaves, explainability tools, human oversight, bias auditing, incident response structures, and sandbox or adversarial pilot testing. The point is simple enough: if the model touches regulated workflows, you need auditable controls around it, not just a clever prompt.
paragraphs_html: Most of the framework sits upstream of deployment. The paper tells firms to classify data sensitivity, check privacy law and data-localisation constraints, and decide what processing is allowed before model selection turns into procurement theatre. In finance, that order is not bureaucratic fussiness. It is the difference between a pilot and a formal incident.

The safeguard layer is broad but concrete. The paper lists controls such as data minimisation, federated learning, anonymisation, encryption, secure enclaves, explainability tools, human oversight, bias auditing, incident response structures, and sandbox or adversarial pilot testing. The point is simple enough: if the model touches regulated workflows, you need auditable controls around it, not just a clever prompt.

sections.label: What deployment should look like
paragraphs: The most practical recommendation is also the least glamorous. The paper favours hybrid setups for many real financial use cases: let the LLM handle interpretation, summarisation, or triage, then hand final compliance-critical decisions to deterministic systems or human reviewers. That preserves the language flexibility firms want without pretending that black-box outputs are good enough for accountability.

The final decisions in the framework force an old-fashioned question that AI teams often dodge, namely whether the gain justifies the spend. More capable models bring higher compute, API, storage, and monitoring costs. Open-source options offer more control, while proprietary services can be quicker to ship but create lock-in and policy risk. After deployment, the authors still want continuous monitoring across performance, trust and safety, operational efficiency, user experience, and risk management, with humans retaining final responsibility when the stakes are high.

The paper favours hybrid setups for many real financial use cases: let the LLM handle interpretation, summarisation, or triage, then hand final compliance-critical decisions to deterministic systems or human reviewers. That preserves the language flexibility firms want without pretending that black-box outputs are good enough for accountability.

The final decisions in the framework force an old-fashioned question that AI teams often dodge, namely whether the gain justifies the spend. More capable models bring higher compute, API, storage, and monitoring costs. Open-source options offer more control, while proprietary services can be quicker to ship but create lock-in and policy risk. After deployment, the authors still want continuous monitoring across performance, trust and safety, operational efficiency, user experience, and risk management, with humans retaining final responsibility when the stakes are high.

references: Tavasoli, A., Sharbaf, M., & Madani, S. M. (2025). Responsible innovation: A strategic framework for financial LLM integration. arXiv. https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.02165
